question: Which of the following physical quantities is fundamental in the International System of Units (SI)?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to identify which quantity belongs to the seven fundamental (base) quantities in the SI system.
explanation.principle: Fundamental quantities are independent physical quantities from which all other physical quantities are derived. The SI base quantities are length, mass, time, electric current, thermodynamic temperature, amount of substance, and luminous intensity.
steps: Recall the seven SI base quantities.
Determine whether the quantity can be expressed in terms of other quantities.
Select the quantity that stands alone as a base quantity.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Density is a derived quantity because it is defined as mass per unit volume (kg m⁻³).
B. Force is a derived quantity because it is obtained from mass × acceleration (kg m s⁻²).
D. Pressure is a derived quantity because it is force per unit area (N m⁻²).
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests the distinction between fundamental and derived quantities. Memorize the seven SI base quantities and their units.
explanation.simple_explanation: Mass is one of the basic quantities used in Physics. Other quantities such as force, density, and pressure are calculated from combinations of these basic quantities. Therefore, mass is fundamental.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Measurements, Units and Dimensions → Fundamental and Derived Quantities.
